questions.q: In which scientific field is E. K. Janaki Ammal most celebrated?
questions.explanation: Janaki Ammal was a pioneering botanist and cytogeneticist known for her study of plant chromosomes.

questions.q: E. K. Janaki Ammal was born in 1897 in which present-day Indian state?
questions.explanation: She was born in Tellicherry (Thalassery) in the Malabar region of present-day Kerala in 1897.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal's well-known work on sugarcane focused on improving which trait of the crop?
questions.explanation: Her cytogenetic work on sugarcane crosses contributed to developing higher-yielding, sweeter and hardier canes suited to Indian conditions.

questions.q: A flower has been named 'Magnolia kobus Janaki Ammal' in her honour. What kind of plant is a magnolia?
questions.explanation: Magnolia is a genus of flowering trees and shrubs, and a magnolia variety was named in Janaki Ammal's honour.

questions.q: What was Janaki Ammal's primary tool of study within cells?
questions.explanation: She specialised in cytogenetics, studying the chromosomes of plants to understand their breeding and evolution.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal is widely remembered as one of the first Indian women to do what in science?
options: Earn a PhD in botany from a US university
questions.explanation: She was among the first Indian women to earn a doctorate (PhD) in botany, which she obtained from the University of Michigan in 1931.

questions.q: The sugarcane she helped improve is mainly grown to produce what?
questions.explanation: Sugarcane is the principal raw material for sugar, and Janaki Ammal's breeding work aimed at better sugar-yielding canes.

questions.q: In which century did E. K. Janaki Ammal carry out most of her scientific career?
questions.explanation: Born in 1897 and active until her death in 1984, her career spanned the 20th century.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal worked extensively with which type of organisms?
questions.explanation: She was a botanist who studied a wide range of plants including sugarcane, eggplant and various ornamental and economic species.

questions.q: What does the 'E. K.' stand for in E. K. Janaki Ammal's name?
questions.explanation: The initials 'E. K.' stand for her family name, Edavalath Kakkat, following Kerala naming convention.

questions.q: At which famous Indian sugarcane research station did Janaki Ammal do pioneering cane-breeding work?
questions.explanation: She worked at the Sugarcane Breeding Institute (then Imperial Sugarcane Breeding Station) in Coimbatore on intergeneric and interspecific cane crosses.

questions.q: From which American university did Janaki Ammal receive her doctoral degree?
questions.explanation: She earned her PhD (1931) from the University of Michigan, which later also awarded her an honorary LLD.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal co-authored the influential 'Chromosome Atlas of Cultivated Plants' with which British biologist?
questions.explanation: She co-wrote the 'Chromosome Atlas of Cultivated Plants' (1945) with cytologist C. D. Darlington while in England.

questions.q: At which renowned British garden did Janaki Ammal work as a cytologist in the 1940s?
options: Royal Horticultural Society's garden at Wisley
questions.explanation: She served as a cytologist at the Royal Horticultural Society's garden at Wisley, where she studied chromosomes of garden plants such as magnolias.

questions.q: Which Prime Minister invited Janaki Ammal to return to India to help reorganise the Botanical Survey of India?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru invited her back to India around 1951 to reorganise and lead the Botanical Survey of India.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal became the first woman to serve as Director-General of which Indian organisation?
options: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
questions.explanation: She was appointed Director-General of the Botanical Survey of India in the early 1950s, reorganising it after independence.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal opposed which hydroelectric project to protect a biodiverse forest in Kerala?
questions.explanation: Late in life she joined the movement against the Silent Valley hydroelectric project to save the tropical rainforest in Kerala.

questions.q: In which year did Janaki Ammal pass away?
questions.explanation: Janaki Ammal died in 1984 in Chennai (then Madras), reportedly while still working in her laboratory.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal's interspecific and intergeneric crosses are an example of which plant breeding aim?
options: Removing all chromosomes from a plant
Combining desirable traits from different species/genera
questions.explanation: By crossing different species and genera (such as sugarcane with related grasses), she sought to combine useful traits like vigour and sugar content.

questions.q: Which honour did the Government of India confer on Janaki Ammal in 1977?
questions.explanation: She was awarded the Padma Shri, one of India's highest civilian honours, in 1977.

questions.q: Besides sugarcane, Janaki Ammal also studied the cytology and geographic distribution of which staple/vegetable crop?
questions.explanation: Her cytogenetic studies extended to eggplant (Solanum) and many other economic and ornamental plants beyond sugarcane.

questions.q: What was unusual about Janaki Ammal's personal life that reflected her dedication to science in early 20th-century India?
options: She gave up science after marriage
She never married and pursued a lifelong research career
She worked only as an administrator
questions.explanation: She remained unmarried and devoted herself fully to botanical research at a time when that was uncommon for Indian women.

questions.q: After returning from the United States, Janaki Ammal taught botany as a professor at which Madras (Chennai) college?
questions.explanation: After earning her degrees at the University of Michigan she returned to India and served as a professor of botany at Women's Christian College in Madras (Chennai). (She had earlier been a student at Queen Mary's College, Madras.)

questions.q: Janaki Ammal received a Barbour Scholarship that enabled her advanced studies. That scholarship was associated with which university?
questions.explanation: The Barbour Scholarship, awarded to Oriental women at the University of Michigan, supported her doctoral studies there.

questions.q: A hybrid sugarcane cane developed at Coimbatore in Janaki Ammal's era is famously prefixed with which code letters?
questions.explanation: Sugarcane varieties bred at the Coimbatore station are designated with the prefix 'Co', reflecting the institute where she worked on cane cytogenetics.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal's cytological research on woody plants at Wisley/RHS is especially linked to chromosome counts in which ornamental genus?
questions.explanation: Her chromosome studies of magnolias at the RHS are why a magnolia variety, Magnolia kobus 'Janaki Ammal', bears her name.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal also conducted ethnobotanical and chromosome-survey work documenting plants used by which group?
questions.explanation: Later in her career she documented the chromosomes, geography and traditional plant uses of medicinal and food plants, including those used by tribal communities.

questions.q: The institute dedicated to her memory, the EKJ Centre / Janaki Ammal national award, recognises work in which area?
questions.explanation: The E. K. Janaki Ammal National Award honours outstanding contributions in plant and animal taxonomy and conservation, reflecting her botanical legacy.

questions.q: Polyploidy, a concept central to Janaki Ammal's cytogenetic work, refers to cells having what?
options: More than two complete sets of chromosomes
questions.explanation: Polyploidy means having more than two complete chromosome sets, a phenomenon she studied to explain plant vigour, size and breeding behaviour.

questions.q: Janaki Ammal's career is often cited as a milestone because she combined frontier cytogenetics with what broader cause?
questions.explanation: Beyond laboratory genetics, she championed the conservation of India's native plants and forests, notably opposing the Silent Valley project.
